skill.name: dependency-management-deps-audit
skill.description: Audit project dependencies for known vulnerabilities, license issues, outdated packages, supply-chain risks, and remediation options.
skill.summary: Find dependency security, license, and maintenance risks with prioritized remediation guidance.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review shows the strongest signals are documentation examples in Markdown, not executable skill code. No prompt injection, credential exfiltration, or malicious endpoint was found; however, the playbook recommends network scans and automated dependency changes, so publication should include a caution about user approval and staging validation.

medium_findings.title: Automated Dependency Remediation Commands Need Approval
medium_findings.description: The playbook documents commands that can change dependency trees, force audit fixes, install packages, and revert files. This is appropriate for a dependency audit skill, but agents should request user approval and run changes in a branch or staging environment.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documented commands directly modify dependencies or run package tooling. The context is legitimate remediation guidance, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Network Calls to Package and Metadata Services
medium_findings.description: The examples contact public package registries and bundle metadata services to gather vulnerability and size data. This matches the skill purpose, but users should understand that package names and versions may be sent to external services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network calls are explicit and target normal dependency-audit services. I found no evidence that secrets or repository contents are sent.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Shell Backticks
low_findings.description: Many external-command detections point to Markdown fenced code blocks or resource links instead of runtime command execution. These are documentation examples and are not executable by the skill itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged backticks are Markdown formatting and resource references. No Ruby execution context or runtime shell wrapper is present in these files.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Scanner Hits Are Dependency Text
low_findings.description: The high-risk weak-crypto findings are false positives caused by words such as security, advisory fields, or remote code execution classification text. No MD5, SHA1 hashing implementation, or crypto use was found in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review found descriptive vulnerability fields and scanning logic, not cryptographic algorithms. The static label does not match the surrounding content.

low_findings.title: Filesystem and Reconnaissance Detections Are Benign Context
low_findings.description: The hard-link and reconnaissance findings do not correspond to hard-link creation or network probing. They occur inside vulnerability data iteration and checklist text rather than filesystem or reconnaissance behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines contain loop logic and a testing checklist item. I found no hard-link command, port scan, host discovery, or similar reconnaissance instruction.

dangerous_patterns.title: Forced Package Audit Fix
dangerous_patterns.description: The remediation script includes a forced npm audit fix. This may introduce breaking dependency changes and should only run after review.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command is explicit and known to alter dependency versions aggressively. It is useful but risky without user approval.

dangerous_patterns.title: Workflow Command Substitution
dangerous_patterns.description: The GitHub Actions example uses shell command substitution to inspect audit results. This is a normal CI pattern, but it is still command execution in generated workflow guidance.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command substitution is present in a CI documentation example. It does not show attacker-controlled input, so command-injection confidence is moderate.

content.value_statement: Dependency risk is hard to prioritize across vulnerabilities, licenses, outdated packages, and supply-chain signals. This skill structures the audit and turns results into remediation steps for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.
actual_capabilities: Inventories dependencies across common package managers and manifest files.
Checks vulnerability data and ranks issues by severity and exposure.
Reviews license compatibility and flags restrictive or unknown licenses.
Prioritizes outdated dependency updates by security impact and effort.
Identifies supply-chain risks such as typosquatting and maintainer changes.
Drafts remediation scripts, pull request notes, and monitoring workflows.
limitations: Requires access to dependency manifests, lockfiles, or package metadata.
May need network access to query registries and vulnerability databases.
Automated fixes can introduce breaking changes and require testing.
Legal license conclusions should be reviewed by qualified counsel.

use_cases.title: Pre-release Dependency Review
use_cases.description: Review dependency vulnerabilities, license risk, and outdated packages before a production release.

use_cases.title: Open Source Maintenance Triage
use_cases.description: Prioritize dependency updates and prepare clear remediation notes for maintainers.

use_cases.title: Compliance Evidence Preparation
use_cases.description: Create a dependency risk summary with license findings and remediation status for audit evidence.

prompt_templates.title: Scan Project Dependencies
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a broad audit.
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit this project dependency set. Identify vulnerable, outdated, and risky packages. Summarize the highest priority fixes first.

prompt_templates.title: Review License Compliance
prompt_templates.scenario: Focused review for legal and policy risk.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the dependency licenses in this project against our project license. Flag incompatible, restrictive, and unknown licenses with recommended next steps.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Safe Updates
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a prioritized dependency update plan. Include security impact, breaking-change risk, test scope, and rollback notes for each high-priority update.

prompt_templates.title: Design Continuous Monitoring
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a dependency monitoring workflow for this repository. Include CI checks, alert thresholds, review ownership, and a safe process for automated remediation.

output_examples.input: Audit a Node.js service before release.
output: Executive risk summary with critical and high vulnerabilities first.
Prioritized update plan with test and rollback notes.
License and bundle-size issues that need review.

output_examples.input: Check dependencies for an internal compliance review.
output: License distribution summary with incompatible packages highlighted.
Remediation recommendations suitable for audit tracking.
CI workflow outline for vulnerability and license checks.
Alerting rules for critical dependency risk.
Pull request checklist for dependency updates.

best_practices: Run automated remediation only in a branch or staging environment.
Validate package updates with tests, lockfile review, and release notes.
Document accepted risks, owners, and target remediation dates.
anti_patterns: Applying forced dependency fixes directly to a production branch.
Ignoring license findings because no vulnerability is present.
Treating scanner severity as priority without checking exploitability and exposure.

faq.question: Does this skill run scanners automatically?
faq.answer: It provides workflows and examples. Tool execution depends on the AI environment and should require user approval.

faq.question: Can it replace a security engineer?
faq.answer: No. It organizes dependency evidence and recommendations, but human review is needed for risk acceptance and release decisions.

faq.question: Which package ecosystems are covered?
faq.answer: The playbook covers common npm, Python, Ruby, Java, Go, Rust, PHP, and .NET dependency files.

faq.question: Will it detect license compliance issues?
faq.answer: It can flag likely compatibility and restriction issues. Legal conclusions should be reviewed by qualified counsel.

faq.question: Does it send data to external services?
faq.answer: Some example workflows query package registries and metadata services. Review network use before scanning private dependency names.

faq.question: How should remediation be handled?
faq.answer: Use a branch, run tests, review lockfile changes, and document any accepted residual risk.
